characters.description: Athenian philosopher. Never wrote anything. Central figure in all texts in this corpus. Son of Sophroniscus (stonemason) and Phaenarete (midwife). Executed by hemlock after trial for impiety and corrupting the youth.

Athenian philosopher, student of Socrates, founder of the Academy. Author of 28 dialogues in this corpus. Never appears as a character in his own dialogues (mentioned once in the Apology as present at the trial).
Wealthy Athenian, close friend of Socrates from the same deme (Alopece). Offered to help Socrates escape from prison.
Athenian religious expert (mantis) who was prosecuting his own father for murder, encountered by Socrates before the trial.
From Elis, captured in war and enslaved in Athens. Freed through Socrates' circle. Narrator of Socrates' final conversation and death.
Theban philosopher, student of Philolaus the Pythagorean. Present at Socrates' death.
Theban philosopher, student of Philolaus. Present at Socrates' death. Raises the strongest objections to the immortality of the soul.
Thessalian aristocrat and military commander. Student of Gorgias. Later served under Cyrus the Younger and was captured by the Persians.
Sophist from Abdera. Most famous of the sophists. Known for 'Man is the measure of all things.' Charged exorbitant fees for teaching.
Rhetorician and sophist from Leontini (Sicily). Famous for his ornate rhetorical style and his claim that rhetoric is the greatest of arts.
Athenian aristocrat who argues that natural justice means the strong should dominate the weak. May be a fictional character created by Plato.
Student of Gorgias from Agrigentum. Argues that rhetoric gives power and that tyrants are happy.
Sophist from Chalcedon. Famous for his aggressive argument that justice is the interest of the stronger.
Plato's older brother. Primary interlocutor in the Republic. Presents the Ring of Gyges thought experiment.
Plato's older brother. Interlocutor in the Republic, raises objections about poetry's effect on character.
Brilliant, controversial Athenian statesman and general. Ward of Pericles. His speech in the Symposium reveals his complex relationship with Socrates.
Athenian tragic poet. Host of the drinking party in Plato's Symposium, celebrating his first victory at the Lenaea.
Priestess from Mantinea. Teacher of Socrates in the art of love, as reported by Socrates in the Symposium. Possibly fictional.
Athenian aristocrat, enthusiast of rhetoric and speeches. Appears in both the Phaedrus and the Symposium.
Young Athenian mathematician. Interlocutor in both the Theaetetus and the Sophist. Made important contributions to geometry.
Pre-Socratic philosopher from Elea. Founder of the Eleatic school. Argues for the unity and unchangeability of Being. In the dialogue, he cross-examines the young Socrates about the theory of Forms.
Unnamed philosopher from Elea, follower of Parmenides. Leads the discussions in the Sophist and Statesman.
Philosopher-statesman from Locri (southern Italy). Pythagorean thinker. Delivers the cosmological account in the Timaeus.
Athenian politician, poet, and philosopher. Plato's mother's cousin. Later became one of the Thirty Tyrants. Narrator of the Atlantis story.
Sophist from Elis. Polymath who claimed expertise in every subject. Known for prodigious memory and practical skills.
Rhapsode from Ephesus, specialist in reciting Homer. His craft raises questions about the nature of knowledge and divine inspiration.
Athenian general known for bravery. Killed at the Battle of Mantinea. Discusses the nature of courage with Socrates.
Athenian general and statesman. Led the disastrous Sicilian Expedition. Discusses courage in the Laches.
Plato's uncle. Handsome young Athenian aristocrat. Later became one of the Thirty Tyrants. Discusses temperance with Socrates.
Young Athenian boy. Discusses friendship with Socrates in a wrestling school.
Sophist from Chios. Along with his brother Dionysodorus, demonstrates eristic (competitive) argumentation.
Son of Hipponicus, brother of Callias. Interlocutor in the Cratylus and companion of Socrates in Xenophon's works.
Follower of Heraclitus. Argues that names have a natural correctness. Later tradition says he was an early teacher of Plato.
Wealthy Athenian politician and tanner. One of Socrates' three accusers at trial. Appears briefly in the Meno.
Young Athenian poet. The primary accuser of Socrates. Cross-examined by Socrates in the Apology.
Old Athenian farmer, protagonist of Aristophanes' Clouds. Enrolls in Socrates' 'Thinkery' to learn how to avoid paying debts.
Son of Strepsiades in the Clouds. His horse-racing debts drive the plot. Eventually learns the 'Unjust Argument' and beats his father.
Son of Callias. Takes over from Philebus as Socrates' interlocutor on whether pleasure or knowledge constitutes the good life.
Associate of both Socrates and Thrasymachus. In the dialogue bearing his name, he criticizes Socrates for being merely protreptic without providing positive doctrine.
Devoted follower of Socrates. Narrator of Plato's Symposium. Present at Socrates' death in the Phaedo. Known for his emotional temperament.
Wealthy Athenian gentleman farmer. Primary interlocutor in the second half of Xenophon's Oeconomicus, where he describes ideal household management.
Son of Crito, friend of Socrates. Interlocutor in Xenophon's Oeconomicus and present in the Symposium.
Young Athenian. Appears in the dialogue bearing his name and in the Lysis. Socrates delivers a mock funeral oration for his benefit.
